questions.prompt: Grammar — Passive voice: "The winning essay ___ by the committee next Friday."
questions.explanation: "Next Friday" points to the future and the essay (not the committee) is the grammatical subject, so we need the future passive: will be announced. "announces" is active present. "is announcing" is an active present continuous (the essay can't announce itself). "was announced" is past — wrong time frame.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Third conditional: "If she had known about the meeting, she ___ on time."
questions.explanation: The if-clause has had known (past perfect), so the result clause requires would have + participle: she would have arrived. "would arrive" is the second conditional result (imaginary present/future). "had arrived" would make a second if-clause, not a result. "arrives" is the zero conditional (fact), which doesn't fit an impossible past.

questions.prompt: Vocabulary — Choose the best word: "After hours of debate, the two sides finally reached an ___."
questions.explanation: You reach an agreement when two parties settle on a shared position after debate: a classic B2 collocation. An argument is the debate itself (the opposite of resolution). An arrival is a physical coming-somewhere event. An appointment is a scheduled meeting — not a consensus outcome.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Gerunds & infinitives: "Would you mind ___ the window? It's very cold."
questions.explanation: mind is always followed by a gerund (-ing): Would you mind closing…? "to close" (infinitive) is wrong after mind — compare with would like, which takes the infinitive. "close" (bare infinitive) follows modals, not mind. "closed" is the past participle and has no function here.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Past perfect: "She realised she ___ her passport at home when she reached the check-in desk."
questions.explanation: She realised (past simple) = the later event. The leaving happened before that, so it needs the past perfect: she had left her passport at home. "left" (past simple) implies the two events happened at the same time, blurring the sequence. "has left" is present perfect — wrong in a past narrative. "was leaving" is past continuous, which sets a background scene, not a completed prior action.

questions.prompt: Spelling — Choose the correctly spelled word: "The company's profits have ___ dramatically this quarter."
questions.explanation: rise is irregular: rise → rose (past simple) → risen (past participle). After have we need the participle: have risen. "rised" is not a word (rise is irregular, no -d ending). "rose" is the past simple, which doesn't follow have. "arisen" is the participle of arise (a different verb — difficulties arise, not profits).
